channel.title: Tourism on Alien Truth Social
channel.description: Recent content in Tourism on Alien Truth Social

item.title: Puerto Rico Highway 303
item.description:
Puerto Rico Highway 303 is a rural road connecting Cabo Rojo and Lajas. A section of this highway, spanning the southern border of Sierra Bermeja, is known as the “Puerto Rico Alien Route” due to numerous UFO reports in the area. While a sign designating it as the “Ruta Extraterrestre” was installed, it was removed after the Puerto Rico Department of Transportation and Public Works rejected official recognition. The municipality of Lajas and local businesses now promote this route as a tourist attraction.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puerto_Rico_Highway_303

item.title: UFO Watchtower
item.description:
The UFO Watchtower, located north of Hooper, Colorado, is an observation platform and campground that opened in May 2000. It was established by cattle farmer Judy Messoline, who converted her barn into a tourist attraction to capitalize on reported UFO sightings in the San Luis Valley. The attraction relies on donations and sales of alien-themed merchandise for funding. Over 30,000 visitors have reportedly visited the Watchtower since its opening, with reported sightings and a “healing garden” also being features.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_Watchtower

item.title: Marfa lights
item.description:
The Marfa lights are an optical phenomenon observed near Marfa, Texas, often seen from a publicized viewing area. Scientific investigations between 2000 and 2008 concluded that these lights are primarily caused by automobile headlights distorted by warm desert air. Historical accounts of the lights date back to 1883, and they have been featured in various media and inspired artistic works.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marfa_lights
